turns.content: I'm stuck on this graph traversal exercise from the book. I tried using Rc<Node> for every node but it's creating circular references that crash my program when I try to insert a parent into a child. Here is my code snippet: let nodes = Vec::new(); let edges = Vec::new(); for id in 0..nodes.len() { let edge = Some((id, parent_id)); edges.push(edge); } println!("Nodes count: {}", nodes.len()); I know petgraph uses graph::GraphMap but I want to avoid that crate and build this myself. Why is Rc causing the stack overflow here?

The issue isn't actually a stack overflow from Rc itself, but rather a fundamental misunderstanding of how ownership works in Rust when dealing with cycles. When you store Node pointers in a Vec and try to link them back via references like &Node, you create a situation where the Vec holds data that contains pointers to the Vec's own elements, which can lead to infinite recursion during borrow checks or runtime panics if not managed carefully. The real solution is to replace Rc<Node> with usize indices representing positions in your nodes vector. This is an arena-like ownership pattern where each node is stored once and referenced by its index position rather than holding a reference that could point back to the container itself.

Ah, so I should just store integers instead of actual Node objects? But what about cloning or moving data between nodes? If I have a tree structure where leaves need to be copied into parent nodes for processing, how do I handle that without Rc? Can't I use RefCell inside the Vec<Node> and then move ownership when needed?

You can absolutely use RefCell for mutable access within your node data structures, but you must never store a RefCell<RefCell<Node>> or similar nested references that create cycles. The key insight is that indices are immutable identifiers while the actual Node contents can still be wrapped in RefCell if they need to change after creation. For example: struct Node { value: i32, children: Vec<usize> } let nodes = vec![Node { value: 10, children: vec![] }, ...]; when you want to update a child's parent relationship, you simply move the index from one node's children vector into another node's parent field. This avoids any borrowing issues because indices don't carry references—they're just numbers. You can still use RefCell inside Node if you need internal mutability without creating circular dependencies.

I see! So my nodes Vec only holds simple structs with usize fields, and I track relationships purely through those integer positions. But what happens when the graph grows dynamically? If I add a new node at index 100, how do I efficiently update all existing references to it without O(n) scans? Also, how does this compare to petgraph's adjacency list implementation if I were to use that crate later?

When you insert a new node into your nodes Vec, its index is determined by its position in the vector, which is O(1) for appending. Any existing references to it are updated simply by adding the new index value to their respective child or parent lists—no scanning required since you're only updating integer fields. Regarding petgraph comparison: that crate uses GraphMap<Node, EdgeList> internally where edges store (u, v) tuples representing indices directly. Your Vec<Node> approach with separate edges Vec is essentially implementing a similar adjacency list pattern but manually organized into two containers instead of one unified graph structure. The main advantage you gain by building this yourself is full control over memory layout and the ability to implement custom traversal strategies like BFS or DFS without being locked into petgraph's specific iteration protocols.

That makes sense about the manual adjacency list organization. I just realized another problem though—what if I need to traverse a deep tree recursively? If I use indices, won't my stack overflow happen because Rust doesn't optimize tail calls or have small integer optimizations like some languages do with Rc pointers? Can I simulate recursion with an explicit Vec<usize> for the traversal path instead of relying on stack frames?

You are absolutely correct to worry about stack depth in recursive graph traversals, and yes, Rust has no automatic tail call optimization or special handling for small integer indices that would prevent deep recursion. However, the solution is exactly what you suggested: replace recursive calls with an explicit Vec<usize> as your traversal path. This manual stack approach allows you to implement DFS or BFS without hitting stack limits regardless of tree depth. For instance: let mut path = vec![]; dfs_recursive(0, &mut path); fn dfs_recursive(current_idx: usize, path: &mut Vec<usize>) { if current_idx == target { return } path.push(current_idx); for child in nodes[current_idx].children.iter() { dfs_recursive(child, path); } path.pop(); } This pattern keeps your recursion logic out of the language stack entirely while maintaining the same traversal behavior.

Perfect, so I'm building a manual stack using Vec<usize> instead of relying on Rust's call stack. One last thing—what if I want to serialize this graph structure to JSON? Since I'm not using petgraph, how do I represent my custom Node and edges Vecs in serde_json::Value format without exposing the internal indices? Do I need to implement a custom Serialize trait for each struct or can I just flatten everything into a single map of index-to-node mappings?

You don't necessarily need complex custom Serialize implementations if you design your serialization carefully. Instead of storing raw Vec<Node> and Vec<Edge>, consider creating wrapper types like GraphData { nodes: Vec<JsonNode>, edges: Vec<(usize, usize)> } where JsonNode implements Serialize with only the fields you want exposed (like value, not internal structure). For JSON output, you can flatten it into a single Map format using serde_json::Map or HashMap<u64, Node> where keys are indices converted to string representations. This avoids exposing internal index logic while still allowing external consumers to reconstruct the graph. You'll need to implement Serialize for your wrapper struct and ensure each node variant in your enum is properly tagged so deserialization remains deterministic.

Got it, flattening into a Map with string keys seems cleaner than nested vectors. I think I understand the core difference now: using indices removes the cyclic reference problem entirely because there's no way for one element to point back to the container itself through references. That's exactly why beginners make this mistake by thinking Rc is enough—they forget that ownership semantics matter more than just referencing data.

Exactly, and that's the crucial lesson here. Beginners often assume that as long as they use smart pointers like Rc or RefCell, they've solved the memory safety problem. But in cyclic graph structures, the real danger isn't the pointer type itself—it's whether the ownership model allows data to flow back into its own container without violating borrow rules. By switching from Rc<Node> to Vec<usize> with separate edge storage, you eliminate the possibility of circular references entirely because indices are just numbers, not pointers that can form cycles. This pattern is essentially what petgraph implements under the hood, but giving you full control over how those indices are managed and serialized without depending on third-party crate abstractions.

Thanks for breaking down the ownership vs reference distinction so clearly. I'll refactor my code to use usize indices now instead of holding node references directly. This approach will definitely prevent the cyclic borrow errors I was hitting before.

You're very welcome! That refactoring should resolve your immediate compilation errors and give you a much more robust foundation for implementing graph algorithms later on. The index-based pattern is particularly well-suited for systems programming contexts where you want fine-grained control over memory layout and borrowing rules without relying on the overhead of smart pointer boxing.
